summary: The USMCA review dominates: USTR is sending negotiators to Mexico as Jamieson Greer pushes to shrink the US trade deficit, while Sheinbaum wants a deal before September despite US tariff and quota demands. BlackRock says the review brings uncertainty but won't cost Mexico its investment grade. Meanwhile Hacienda is cutting productive investment to control the fiscal deficit, Pemex halted oil exports to Cuba after US pressure, and former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo was arrested on organized crime charges tied to fuel theft.

lead.h1: USTR delegation to visit Mexico as US pushes to cut trade deficit
lead.context: Jamieson Greer says the US wants to shrink its trade deficit with Mexico as USTR sends technical negotiators ahead of the USMCA review, signaling Washington will use the review to press for structural changes in trade flows.
lead.background: USMCA's annual review process, now in place after the US declined a 16-year extension, gives Washington a recurring venue to press changes to trade terms.
lead.drivers: USTR chief Jamieson Greer said he has a presidential mandate to control the US trade deficit with Mexico using tariffs, quotas or other mechanisms, and to tighten rules of origin in manufacturing sectors.
lead.implications: Washington aims to limit growth in Mexican exports to the US and redistribute production toward US territory, framing the deficit as a problem tied to global overcapacity and unfair practices.

items.headline: Sheinbaum says USMCA review enters key phase, seeks deal before September
items.context: Her comments set a rough timeline for resolving the review, a process now complicated by US demands for tariffs and quotas on Mexican exports.
items.background: USMCA moved to annual reviews after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, with existing trade continuing under current rules while future terms remain unsettled.
items.drivers: Sheinbaum said Mexico's strategy is to make the 2026 review the most consequential one, aiming to fix clear rules so future evaluations focus on compliance rather than renegotiation.
items.implications: Success in this review would reduce the risk of repeated renegotiations or new trade measures in subsequent annual checks.
items.next: A USTR delegation visits Mexico the following week to continue negotiations, with Mexico seeking agreements before September.

items.headline: BlackRock says Mexico will keep investment grade despite USMCA review risk
items.context: The asset manager flagged that the annual USMCA review process could bring uncertainty for investors but said a downgrade of Mexico's sovereign rating remains unlikely.

items.headline: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
items.context: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.
items.background: Ernesto Ruffo was Baja California's governor from 1989 to 1995 and later founded Ingemar, a company he has described as handling only customs paperwork.
items.drivers: Prosecutors say the arrest stems from a high complexity investigation into Ingemar, and security forces detained business partner Ricardo Thompson Navarro the same day in Ensenada.
items.implications: Ruffo, 74, was transferred in handcuffs to the FGR facility in Tijuana to face organized crime and fuel theft charges.
items.next: Ruffo has been transferred, handcuffed, to FGR facilities in Tijuana.

items.headline: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
items.context: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
items.background: Pemex is Mexico's state oil company and has long supplied crude to Cuba; the Trump administration has pressed Latin American governments over ties to the Diaz-Canel government.
items.drivers: Banxico data show Pemex's exports to Cuba fell to zero in May, a continuous decline since January, amid US pressure and threats from Trump to intervene against Diaz-Canel.
items.next: Sheinbaum said in late June that Mexico will keep sending petroleum products despite Trump's threats.

items.headline: BlackRock says annual USMCA reviews will bring uncertainty to Mexico investment
items.context: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose its sovereign investment-grade rating, but flags the treaty's annual review mechanism as a recurring source of uncertainty for investors in the country.
items.background: USMCA's first joint review in July 2026 ended without the 16-year extension Mexico sought, moving the treaty to yearly reviews under Article 34.7 while existing trade continues.
items.drivers: BlackRock's José Luis Ortega said long-term capital will not commit aggressively while the annual-review mechanism keeps future trade rules uncertain.
items.implications: The firm does not expect high economic growth this year because investment is unlikely to arrive at scale under continued uncertainty.

items.headline: Finance ministry accelerates cuts to productive investment to control fiscal deficit
items.context: Hacienda is cutting spending on productive investment ahead of the second half of the year in an effort to contain the fiscal deficit, according to the report.
items.background: Mexico's finance ministry, Hacienda, manages public spending as broad debt has risen and Pemex's financial support draws on federal capacity that could otherwise fund infrastructure.
items.drivers: Despite stronger SAT tax collection, Hacienda is cutting physical investment in Pemex to ease the fiscal deficit, with capital spending on new machinery down 45.7% in real terms over five months.
items.implications: Physical investment in Pemex fell to its lowest level since 2008, even as social spending continues to rise, showing fiscal consolidation is coming from productive investment rather than social programs.

items.headline: Rocha Moya case and organized crime complicate USMCA outlook
items.context: The decision to hold annual reviews instead of extending the deal for 16 years is read as political pressure from the Trump administration tied to corruption and cartel cases implicating Mexican officials.
items.background: Rubén Rocha Moya, former governor of Sinaloa, is at the center of a case tied to organized crime that has strained US-Mexico security relations.
items.drivers: Analyst David Saucedo said the shift to annual USMCA reviews instead of a 16-year extension could reflect Trump administration retaliation over inaction on the Rocha Moya case.
items.implications: Security tensions, including narcotrafficking cooperation and Washington's view of Mexico's response to organized crime, are shaping the bilateral trade relationship alongside economic factors.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.44 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
